text:
if you break down the katakana that they always used on official publications, the pronuncation is roughly "gee-roo-gah-mesh-shoo"
i don't think many Americans could pronounce it the Japanese way if they tried

so it makes more sense to just say it the English way
"guh-roo-guh-mesh" is an English mispronunciation of a Japanese word that is already a mispronunciation of an English word that is probably a mispronunciation of a Mesopotamian word to begin with

so, simplest solution:
Gilgamesh.

text:
I already posted this but I replied to the wrong guy,
"sakura-con ni ike!" would be the best translation to "get your ass to sakura con!" IKE is the strong imperative form of 'ikimasu' and is stronger than the general imperative, "sakura con ni itte". "get your ass to sakura con" would not make much sense to a japanese person if it were translated literally.

text:
Bottom line: Japanese is a vague language.  

The guy did not say "I will go to Sakura Con," he literally said what SakuraConPR wrote.  It is understood, in the context of the vid, that he meant something like the subtitle: "get your butts to sakura con."  There is no good reason i can see why one would think he wanted to go to sakura con.

No language should ever be translated literally, it often sounds silly.  Not every language was created equal, and this becomes very obvious in translation.

text:
The way you criticize the Japanese gives me the impression that you are not a native speaker, but rather someone who has picked up the language and learned it for some period of time.

The biggest implication of this is the fact that Japanese speakers almost always prefer to use へ before using に, whereas gaijin typically prefer to use に first because the latter particle can be used for more things than just "direction toward."
